name: Singleton
intent: Ensure a class has only one instance and provide a global point of access to it.
whenToUse: Use when exactly one object is needed to coordinate actions across a system — such as a configuration manager, connection pool, or logging service. Avoid when testability is critical, as global state makes unit testing difficult.

participants.name: Singleton
participants.role: Defines the getInstance() operation that lets clients access its unique instance. May be responsible for creating its own unique instance.

realWorldJava: java.lang.Runtime.getRuntime() returns the single Runtime instance for the JVM. Spring's ApplicationContext acts as a Singleton container — each bean is created once and shared. java.awt.Desktop.getDesktop() follows the same pattern.
pros: Guarantees a single instance, preventing conflicting state
Provides a global access point without passing references everywhere
Instance is created only when first needed (lazy initialization)
cons: Global state makes unit testing difficult — hard to swap with a mock
Violates Single Responsibility Principle by managing its own lifecycle
Can mask bad design by acting as a dumping ground for unrelated functionality
Requires careful synchronization in multithreaded environments
interviewTips: Always mention the double-checked locking pattern AND the enum variant — interviewers expect you to know both
Explain why 'volatile' is required: without it, the JVM may reorder instructions causing a partially-initialized object to be seen by another thread
Be ready to discuss why Singleton is sometimes considered an anti-pattern (global state, tight coupling, testing difficulty)
Enum Singleton handles serialization and reflection attacks automatically — highlight this over classic DCL

name: Builder
intent: Separate the construction of a complex object from its representation so that the same construction process can create different representations.
whenToUse: Use when constructing complex objects that require many optional parameters, or when the construction process must allow different representations of the same object. Avoids telescoping constructors with many nullable parameters.

participants.name: Builder
participants.role: Specifies the abstract interface for creating parts of a Product.

participants.name: ConcreteBuilder
participants.role: Constructs and assembles parts of the product by implementing the Builder interface. Defines and tracks the representation it creates.

participants.name: Director
participants.role: Constructs an object using the Builder interface. Optional in many Java implementations.

participants.name: Product
participants.role: Represents the complex object under construction.

realWorldJava: StringBuilder and StringBuffer are the canonical Java examples — append() returns 'this' enabling method chaining. Lombok's @Builder annotation generates the entire Builder boilerplate at compile time. java.util.stream.Stream.Builder, ProcessBuilder, and OkHttp's Request.Builder all use this pattern. Spring's MockMvcRequestBuilders also follows this approach.
pros: Eliminates telescoping constructors — no need for overloaded constructors with nullable parameters
Immutable Product objects are easy to create (set all fields before constructing)
Construction process is readable and self-documenting due to method chaining
Same builder can produce different representations with different parameter combinations
cons: Increases code volume — requires a separate Builder inner class for each Product
Builder and Product classes can become out of sync if Product fields are added without updating Builder
Mutable builder state can cause issues if the builder is reused across multiple build() calls
interviewTips: Compare Builder to telescoping constructors: 'new Person(name, age, null, null, "NYC", null)' — Builder makes optional parameters explicit
Mention Lombok @Builder as the production shortcut — shows awareness of real-world Java tooling
Highlight that making the Product's constructor private enforces that it can only be built via the Builder
StringBuilder is the most common interview example — it uses the pattern without a separate Director

name: Factory Method
intent: Define an interface for creating an object, but let subclasses decide which class to instantiate. Factory Method lets a class defer instantiation to subclasses.
whenToUse: Use when a class cannot anticipate the class of objects it must create, or when subclasses should control the objects their superclass creates. Common in framework design where the framework calls code it doesn't control.

participants.name: Creator
participants.role: Declares the factory method (createProduct) which returns a Product. May define a default implementation that returns a ConcreteProduct.

participants.name: ConcreteCreator
participants.role: Overrides the factory method to return an instance of ConcreteProduct.

participants.name: Product
participants.role: Defines the interface of objects the factory method creates.

participants.name: ConcreteProduct
participants.role: Implements the Product interface.

realWorldJava: Spring's BeanFactory and ApplicationContext use factory methods to create and manage beans — getBean() is the canonical factory method. java.util.Calendar.getInstance() returns a subclass appropriate for the locale. java.util.Iterator from Collection.iterator() defers the concrete iterator type to each collection implementation.
pros: Eliminates tight coupling between Creator and ConcreteProduct classes
Single Responsibility: product creation logic is centralized in one place
Open/Closed: adding new product types requires only a new ConcreteCreator subclass
cons: Can lead to a parallel class hierarchy — every new ConcreteProduct needs a corresponding ConcreteCreator
Client code must subclass Creator to change the Product type, which may be undesirable
interviewTips: Distinguish Factory Method (subclass decides) from Abstract Factory (interface for families) and Simple Factory (static helper — not a GoF pattern)
Spring's ApplicationContext.getBean() is the most cited real-world Java example — know it cold
Explain that Factory Method is typically combined with Template Method: the template method defines the algorithm, the factory method creates the objects it uses
The pattern enables the Open/Closed Principle — adding a new product type doesn't change existing Creator code

name: Abstract Factory
intent: Provide an interface for creating families of related or dependent objects without specifying their concrete classes.
whenToUse: Use when a system must be independent of how its products are created, or when you need to ensure that a family of related products are always used together. Particularly useful when swapping between product families at runtime (e.g., different database vendors).

participants.name: AbstractFactory
participants.role: Declares an interface for operations that create abstract product objects (one method per product type).

participants.name: ConcreteFactory
participants.role: Implements the operations to create concrete product objects. Each factory corresponds to one product family.

participants.name: AbstractProduct
participants.role: Declares an interface for a type of product object.

participants.name: ConcreteProduct
participants.role: Defines a product object to be created by the corresponding concrete factory; implements the AbstractProduct interface.

realWorldJava: JDBC itself is the classic Abstract Factory in the JDK: java.sql.DriverManager returns a java.sql.Connection (abstract), and each driver (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle) provides its own Connection, Statement, and ResultSet implementations. javax.xml.parsers.DocumentBuilderFactory and SAXParserFactory follow the same pattern — calling newInstance() returns a platform-specific implementation.
pros: Ensures product families are always used together consistently
Client code is isolated from concrete product implementations
Swapping product families requires changing only the factory, not client code
Promotes consistency among products within a family
cons: Adding a new product type requires changing the AbstractFactory interface and ALL concrete factories
Can become overly complex when only a few products are needed
More rigid than Factory Method when the product family is not well-defined upfront
interviewTips: Key distinction: Factory Method creates one product via inheritance; Abstract Factory creates families of products via composition
JDBC is the canonical answer for real-world Java — DriverManager + Connection + Statement + ResultSet is a textbook Abstract Factory family
Mention that Abstract Factory is typically implemented using Factory Methods internally
The pattern is ideal when the system needs to be portable across multiple platforms or database vendors

name: Prototype
intent: Specify the kinds of objects to create using a prototypical instance, and create new objects by copying (cloning) this prototype.
whenToUse: Use when object creation is expensive (e.g., database queries, network calls) and a pre-configured instance can be copied more cheaply. Also useful when you need to create objects whose type is specified at runtime.

participants.name: Prototype
participants.role: Declares an interface for cloning itself (the clone() method).

participants.name: ConcretePrototype
participants.role: Implements the cloning operation.

participants.name: Client
participants.role: Creates a new object by asking a prototype to clone itself.

realWorldJava: Object.clone() is Java's built-in prototype mechanism, but it has pitfalls (Cloneable is a marker interface, not a functional one). Spring's prototype bean scope creates a new bean instance on each getBean() call using this pattern. In practice, copy constructors or static factory methods like Person.copyOf(other) are preferred over Cloneable due to its design flaws (Joshua Bloch, Effective Java Item 13).
pros: Avoids expensive initialization by copying a pre-configured object
Reduces subclassing — clone the prototype instead of creating subclass hierarchies
Clients can produce new objects without knowing their concrete types
cons: Deep cloning complex object graphs is error-prone — every mutable field must be copied recursively
Cloneable interface in Java is poorly designed — clone() is defined on Object, not Cloneable
Circular references in object graphs can cause infinite loops during deep cloning
interviewTips: Always distinguish shallow vs deep copy — this is the most common follow-up question
Explain why Effective Java recommends against Cloneable: clone() is declared on Object, not on Cloneable; it throws checked CloneNotSupportedException; and default super.clone() is a shallow copy
Preferred alternatives: copy constructors (new Person(other)) or static factory methods — these are explicit and type-safe
Spring's prototype scope is the canonical real-world example

name: Decorator
intent: Attach additional responsibilities to an object dynamically. Decorators provide a flexible alternative to subclassing for extending functionality.
whenToUse: Use when you need to add responsibilities to individual objects without affecting others of the same class. Use instead of subclassing when the number of feature combinations would cause a class explosion.

participants.name: Component
participants.role: Defines the interface for objects that can have responsibilities added to them dynamically.

participants.name: ConcreteComponent
participants.role: Defines an object to which additional responsibilities can be attached.

participants.name: Decorator
participants.role: Maintains a reference to a Component object and defines an interface conforming to Component's interface.

participants.name: ConcreteDecorator
participants.role: Adds responsibilities to the Component.

realWorldJava: Java I/O is the canonical example: new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(new FileInputStream("file.txt"))) chains three decorators. Each wraps the previous: FileInputStream reads bytes, InputStreamReader converts to chars, BufferedReader adds buffering. javax.servlet.http.HttpServletRequestWrapper and HttpServletResponseWrapper are Decorators that let filters add behavior. java.util.Collections.synchronizedList() and unmodifiableList() are single-method decorators.
pros: More flexible than subclassing — behaviors can be combined at runtime in any order
Each decorator has a single, focused responsibility (SRP)
New behaviors can be added without modifying existing classes (OCP)
cons: Many small objects are created, which can complicate debugging (deep decorator stacks)
Order of wrapping matters — changing decorator order changes behavior
Identity checks (instanceof, ==) behave unexpectedly with decorated objects
interviewTips: Java I/O is the go-to example — trace FileInputStream -> InputStreamReader -> BufferedInputStream to show the chain
Distinguish Decorator (adds behavior, same interface) from Proxy (controls access, same interface) and Adapter (converts interface)
Note that Collections.synchronizedList() is a single-operation decorator — a simpler variant of the full pattern
The key insight: Decorator wraps the same interface, so the client doesn't know it's talking to a decorator chain

name: Proxy
intent: Provide a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it.
whenToUse: Use when you need lazy initialization (virtual proxy), access control (protection proxy), remote object access (remote proxy), logging/caching without modifying the real subject (smart proxy), or AOP-style cross-cutting concerns.

participants.name: Subject
participants.role: Defines the common interface for RealSubject and Proxy so a proxy can be used anywhere a RealSubject is expected.

participants.name: RealSubject
participants.role: Defines the real object that the proxy represents.

participants.name: Proxy
participants.role: Maintains a reference to the RealSubject. Controls access to it and may be responsible for creating and deleting it.

realWorldJava: Spring AOP uses JDK dynamic proxies (for interface-based classes) and CGLIB proxies (for class-based) to implement @Transactional, @Cacheable, and @Async. Hibernate uses CGLIB proxies for lazy-loaded entity associations — a field annotated @ManyToOne is a proxy until first accessed. java.rmi stubs are remote proxies. Spring Security's SecurityInterceptor is a protection proxy.
pros: Can add cross-cutting concerns (logging, security, caching) without modifying the real subject
Controls access to expensive resources via virtual or remote proxies
Transparent to clients — they interact with the same interface
cons: JDK dynamic proxy requires an interface — cannot proxy concrete classes without CGLIB or ByteBuddy
Adds indirection which can complicate debugging and stack traces
Response time increases slightly due to reflection overhead in JDK proxies
interviewTips: Spring AOP is the most important real-world answer — explain that @Transactional works by wrapping your bean in a proxy that starts/commits/rolls back the transaction around your method
JDK proxy requires an interface; CGLIB subclasses the target class — this explains why @Transactional on final methods doesn't work in Spring
Distinguish Proxy (same interface, controls access) from Decorator (same interface, adds behavior) — Proxy is about control, Decorator is about enhancement
Hibernate's lazy loading is a virtual proxy — the entity proxy is returned immediately, database query happens on first field access

name: Adapter
intent: Convert the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect. Adapter lets classes work together that couldn't otherwise because of incompatible interfaces.
whenToUse: Use when you want to use an existing class but its interface is incompatible with what you need. Common when integrating third-party libraries, legacy code, or when bridging between two systems with different APIs.

participants.name: Target
participants.role: Defines the domain-specific interface that the Client uses.

participants.name: Client
participants.role: Collaborates with objects conforming to the Target interface.

participants.name: Adaptee
participants.role: Defines an existing interface that needs adapting.

participants.name: Adapter
participants.role: Adapts the interface of Adaptee to the Target interface.

realWorldJava: Arrays.asList() adapts an array to the List interface. Collections.unmodifiableList() adapts a mutable List into a read-only List. InputStreamReader adapts an InputStream (bytes) to a Reader (characters). Spring's HandlerAdapter adapts various controller types (@Controller, HttpRequestHandler, Servlet) to a unified interface that DispatcherServlet understands.
pros: Enables collaboration between incompatible interfaces without modifying existing code
Single Responsibility: the adapter isolates the interface conversion logic
Open/Closed: new adapters can be added without changing client or adaptee
cons: Increases complexity — another layer of indirection is added
Sometimes it's cleaner to modify the original class if it is under your control
Class Adapter (via inheritance) works only for single inheritance — Java's object adapter (composition) is generally preferred
interviewTips: Java's InputStreamReader is the classic example — it converts the byte-stream InputStream API to the character-stream Reader API
Distinguish Adapter (converts interfaces) from Facade (simplifies a complex system) and Decorator (adds behavior without interface change)
In Java, prefer Object Adapter (composition) over Class Adapter (multiple inheritance) since Java doesn't support multiple class inheritance
Mention that the Adapter pattern is also called 'Wrapper' — same concept as Collections.unmodifiableList wrapping a List

name: Facade
intent: Provide a unified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem. Facade defines a higher-level interface that makes the subsystem easier to use.
whenToUse: Use when you want to provide a simple interface to a complex subsystem, when you want to layer your subsystems, or when there are many interdependent classes in a subsystem that clients don't need to understand in detail.

participants.name: Facade
participants.role: Knows which subsystem classes are responsible for a request. Delegates client requests to appropriate subsystem objects.

participants.name: Subsystem Classes
participants.role: Implement subsystem functionality. Handle work assigned by the Facade object. Have no knowledge of the facade.

realWorldJava: Spring's JdbcTemplate is the most important Java example — it hides JDBC boilerplate (get connection, create statement, execute, handle ResultSet, close in finally) behind a single query() or update() call. SLF4J is a facade over Log4j, Logback, and java.util.logging — client code calls Logger.info() without knowing which logging backend is used. javax.faces.context.FacesContext is another framework-level facade.
pros: Simplifies usage of complex subsystems — clients only need to know the Facade
Decouples clients from subsystem internals — subsystem can change without affecting clients
Promotes weak coupling between subsystems and their clients
cons: Risk of becoming a 'god object' — the Facade can grow too large and absorb too much responsibility
Does not prevent clients from using subsystem classes directly when they need fine-grained control
Can hide useful subsystem functionality behind an overly simplified API
interviewTips: JdbcTemplate is the canonical answer — describe how it eliminates 10+ lines of try/catch/finally boilerplate in favor of a single query() call
SLF4J as a facade over logging backends is another excellent example — cite it for logging-related questions
Distinguish Facade (simplifies a subsystem) from Adapter (converts one interface to another) — both wrap other objects but for different reasons
Facade doesn't prevent access to the subsystem; it just provides a convenience layer

name: Composite
intent: Compose objects into tree structures to represent part-whole hierarchies. Composite lets clients treat individual objects and compositions of objects uniformly.
whenToUse: Use when you need to represent hierarchies of objects (trees), and you want client code to treat leaf nodes and composite nodes identically. Classic examples: UI component trees, file system directories, expression trees.

participants.name: Component
participants.role: Declares the interface for objects in the composition. Optionally implements default behavior common to all classes.

participants.name: Leaf
participants.role: Represents leaf objects in the composition (no children). Defines behavior for primitive objects.

participants.name: Composite
participants.role: Defines behavior for components having children. Stores child components and implements child-related operations.

participants.name: Client
participants.role: Manipulates objects in the composition through the Component interface.

realWorldJava: java.awt.Container extends Component — a Container can hold other Components (including other Containers), making the AWT/Swing hierarchy a Composite. javax.faces.component.UIComponent in JSF works the same way. In Spring's expression language (SpEL), expression nodes form a Composite tree. JSON/XML parsers often model document nodes as Composites.
pros: Clients can treat leaf and composite objects uniformly — no special casing needed
Easy to add new component types — just add a new Leaf or Composite class
Natural fit for hierarchical data structures like file systems, org charts, or UI trees
cons: Can make the design overly general — hard to restrict which components can be children of which
Operations that don't apply to leaves (add/remove children) must still be handled — either by throwing exceptions or providing empty implementations
Deeply nested trees can be slow for operations that recurse the entire tree
interviewTips: File system (File vs Directory) is the cleanest interview example — draw the tree and explain why getSize() works recursively without type-checking
Swing's Component/Container hierarchy is the Java stdlib example — Container.add() takes any Component, including other Containers
Clarify the design decision: should add/remove be on the Component interface (transparency) or only on Composite (safety)?
The key insight: the same method call (getSize, print) works correctly regardless of whether the node is a leaf or a subtree

name: Strategy
intent: Define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each one, and make them interchangeable. Strategy lets the algorithm vary independently from clients that use it.
whenToUse: Use when you have multiple variants of an algorithm and want to switch between them at runtime. Use to eliminate conditionals that select algorithm behavior. Ideal when different clients need different variants of the same behavior.

participants.name: Strategy
participants.role: Declares an interface common to all supported algorithms.

participants.name: ConcreteStrategy
participants.role: Implements the algorithm using the Strategy interface.

participants.name: Context
participants.role: Is configured with a ConcreteStrategy object. Maintains a reference to a Strategy object. May define an interface that lets Strategy access its data.

realWorldJava: java.util.Comparator is the most widely-used Strategy in the JDK — it's a @FunctionalInterface that encapsulates a comparison algorithm, passable to Collections.sort() and List.sort(). java.util.concurrent.ThreadPoolExecutor accepts a RejectedExecutionHandler strategy. Spring Security's AuthenticationStrategy encapsulates session management behavior. javax.servlet.http.HttpServlet's service() dispatches to doGet/doPost strategies.
pros: Eliminates conditional statements that select behavior — replaces if/switch with polymorphism
Strategies can be swapped at runtime without changing the context
Each strategy is independently testable
Open/Closed: new strategies can be added without modifying the context
cons: Clients must be aware of different strategies to choose the right one
Increased number of objects — each strategy requires its own class (mitigated by Java 8 lambdas)
Strategy and Context may share data via interface or parameters, which can create coupling
interviewTips: Comparator is the most important Strategy example in Java — every developer uses it daily without necessarily naming the pattern
Java 8 lambdas are anonymous ConcreteStrategies — this shows you understand how functional interfaces map to GoF patterns
Compare Strategy (composition, runtime swap) vs Template Method (inheritance, compile-time fixed skeleton) — they solve similar problems differently
Mention that Strategy eliminates large if-else chains: instead of 'if sortType == PRICE... else if sortType == RATING...' you have polymorphism

name: Observer
intent: Define a one-to-many dependency between objects so that when one object changes state, all its dependents are notified and updated automatically.
whenToUse: Use when a change to one object requires changing others, and you don't know how many objects need to change. Use for event-driven systems where publishers should not be coupled to specific subscribers.

participants.name: Subject
participants.role: Knows its observers. Provides an interface for attaching and detaching Observer objects.

participants.name: Observer
participants.role: Defines an updating interface for objects that should be notified of changes in a Subject.

participants.name: ConcreteSubject
participants.role: Stores state of interest to ConcreteObserver objects. Sends notification when state changes.

participants.name: ConcreteObserver
participants.role: Maintains a reference to a ConcreteSubject object. Implements the Observer updating interface.

realWorldJava: java.util.EventListener is the Observer interface in AWT/Swing — ActionListener, MouseListener, etc. are all ConcreteObservers. Spring's ApplicationEventPublisher and ApplicationListener implement Observer for application events. java.util.Observable (deprecated in Java 9) was the original GoF implementation. Reactor's Flux/Mono and RxJava's Observable implement a reactive variant of this pattern.
pros: Loose coupling — Subject knows only that observers implement a notification interface
Supports broadcast communication — one change notifies many observers
New observers can be added without modifying the Subject (OCP)
cons: Observers are notified in an unspecified order — order-dependent logic is fragile
Memory leaks if observers are not properly unsubscribed (the 'lapsed listener' problem)
Cascading updates can cause performance issues in deeply connected observer networks
interviewTips: Swing's ActionListener (button.addActionListener(e -> ...)) is the most familiar Java Observer example — every Java developer has used it
Always mention the 'lapsed listener' memory leak — an object that registers as a listener but is never unregistered will be retained by the Subject indefinitely
Spring ApplicationEvents are the enterprise Observer — @EventListener methods are ConcreteObservers registered without explicit subscribe/unsubscribe calls
Reactive streams (Project Reactor, RxJava) are the modern evolution of Observer — adds backpressure and async composition

name: Template Method
intent: Define the skeleton of an algorithm in an operation, deferring some steps to subclasses. Template Method lets subclasses redefine certain steps of an algorithm without changing the algorithm's structure.
whenToUse: Use when multiple classes share the same algorithm structure but differ in specific steps. Use to control which parts of a superclass algorithm subclasses can override. Common in frameworks where the framework defines the skeleton and applications fill in the details.

participants.name: AbstractClass
participants.role: Defines abstract primitive operations that concrete subclasses define to implement steps of an algorithm. Implements a template method defining the skeleton.

participants.name: ConcreteClass
participants.role: Implements the primitive operations to carry out subclass-specific steps of the algorithm.

realWorldJava: Spring's JdbcTemplate.query() is a Template Method — it handles connection acquisition, statement creation, exception mapping, and resource cleanup, while the RowMapper callback fills in the result-mapping step. AbstractList in the JDK defines iterator() and other operations as template methods, requiring subclasses to implement only get(int) and size(). HttpServlet.service() dispatches to doGet()/doPost() — the dispatch skeleton is fixed, the handler methods are the variable steps.
pros: Code reuse — the invariant algorithm skeleton lives in one place (superclass)
Hollywood Principle: 'Don't call us, we'll call you' — framework calls the subclass hooks
Hook methods provide optional extension points without forcing subclasses to override everything
cons: Requires inheritance — clients must subclass to vary behavior, unlike Strategy which uses composition
Inverting control can make the flow harder to follow (call stack jumps between super and subclass)
Liskov Substitution Principle can be violated if subclasses override steps in unexpected ways
interviewTips: JdbcTemplate is the single best answer — it reduces 15 lines of JDBC boilerplate to a 3-line query() call, with the RowMapper as the variable step
Distinguish Template Method (inheritance, algorithm skeleton fixed) from Strategy (composition, entire algorithm replaceable)
Explain the Hollywood Principle: the template calls the hook — subclasses don't call super.export(), the superclass calls subclass.formatRecord()
Hook methods vs abstract methods: hooks are optional extensions with a default impl; abstract methods are mandatory steps the subclass must provide

name: Command
intent: Encapsulate a request as an object, thereby letting you parameterize clients with different requests, queue or log requests, and support undoable operations.
whenToUse: Use when you need to parameterize objects with operations, queue operations for deferred execution, support undo/redo, log operations for auditing, or build transactional systems where operations can be rolled back.

participants.name: Command
participants.role: Declares an interface for executing an operation.

participants.name: ConcreteCommand
participants.role: Defines a binding between a Receiver object and an action. Implements execute() by invoking the corresponding operation(s) on Receiver.

participants.name: Client
participants.role: Creates a ConcreteCommand object and sets its receiver.

participants.name: Invoker
participants.role: Asks the command to carry out the request.

participants.name: Receiver
participants.role: Knows how to perform the operations associated with carrying out a request.

realWorldJava: java.lang.Runnable and java.util.concurrent.Callable are the JDK Command interfaces — they encapsulate a task (the operation) as an object that can be submitted to an ExecutorService (the Invoker). java.util.concurrent.Future represents the result of a Command submitted for async execution. Spring's @Transactional uses a Command-like rollback mechanism. JDBC's PreparedStatement.addBatch() / executeBatch() queues SQL commands for batch execution.
pros: Decouples the object that invokes an operation from the one that knows how to perform it
Commands are first-class objects — they can be queued, logged, serialized, and replayed
Undo/redo is straightforward — each command stores enough state to reverse itself
Composite Commands (macros) can be built by combining simpler commands
cons: Can result in many small Command classes for each distinct operation
The Command must store sufficient state for undo — for large objects this can be memory-intensive
Sequence of commands can be hard to reason about as systems grow
interviewTips: Runnable is the simplest Java Command — it encapsulates 'do something' as an object passable to threads or ExecutorService
The undo/redo stack is the most distinctive feature of Command — make sure you can explain how execute() and undo() make this possible
ExecutorService is the Invoker — it queues Runnable/Callable Commands and decides when to execute them
Mention that JDBC batch operations (addBatch/executeBatch) are a practical application — commands are collected, then fired together for efficiency

name: Chain of Responsibility
intent: Avoid coupling the sender of a request to its receiver by giving more than one object a chance to handle the request. Chain the receiving objects and pass the request along the chain until an object handles it.
whenToUse: Use when more than one object may handle a request and the handler isn't known a priori. Use to issue requests to one of several objects without specifying the receiver explicitly. Common in middleware, filter pipelines, and event-handling systems.

participants.name: Handler
participants.role: Defines an interface for handling requests. Optionally implements the successor link.

participants.name: ConcreteHandler
participants.role: Handles requests it is responsible for. Can access its successor. If it can handle the request, it does so; otherwise it forwards to its successor.

participants.name: Client
participants.role: Initiates the request to a ConcreteHandler object on the chain.

realWorldJava: javax.servlet.Filter and FilterChain are the canonical Java Chain of Responsibility — each Filter calls chain.doFilter() to pass control to the next filter. Spring Security is built on a chain of SecurityFilter instances (UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter, BasicAuthenticationFilter, ExceptionTranslationFilter, etc.) where each handles its specific concern and passes to the next. Java's exception handling (try-catch with multiple catch blocks) is also a form of chain of responsibility.
pros: Decouples sender from receiver — the sender doesn't need to know which handler will process its request
Flexible chain configuration — handlers can be added, removed, or reordered at runtime
Single Responsibility: each handler focuses on one specific concern
cons: No guarantee a request will be handled — it can fall off the end of the chain
Debugging is difficult — hard to trace which handler processed a request in a long chain
Performance degrades with very long chains if most requests must traverse many handlers
interviewTips: Servlet Filter chain is the canonical Java answer — doFilter() is the 'pass to next handler' call; not calling it short-circuits the chain
Spring Security's filter chain is the enterprise example — each filter handles one security concern (auth, CSRF, session management) and the chain is ordered carefully
Distinguish from Strategy: Strategy selects one algorithm from many; CoR passes the request through a sequence, any of which may handle it
The critical design question: should the handler stop the chain after handling, or pass it on regardless? (Servlet filters can do both)
